title: How LEDs Are Making Light Pollution Worse
excerpt: Bad decisions have turned efficient technology into a driver of light pollution. There is a better way
content_html:
The River Thames is aglow with light reflected from the Vauxhall and Nine Elms skyscraper clusters [left] and the illuminated face of the Millbank Tower [right].

In the chill of a London spring night, under overcast skies, iconic Trafalgar Square opens around me. Admiral Nelson rises on his pedestal, the National Gallery rests behind, the church of St Martin-in-the-Fields sits nearby. From the 13th century, the site served as the Royal Mews for hawks and then horses. By 1844, it was a public space at the heart of one of the biggest cities in the world.

Despite the square’s presence through that grand sweep of history, it’s not why I’m here. My interest is far more specific: I want to find out what happens to spaces like this when artificial light, specifically from light-emitting diodes (LEDs), intrudes. My companion tonight is Simon Thorp, a local lighting designer, who crouches in the shadows near Nelson’s spire, light meter in hand. “Two lux,” he reports, “and it’s very comfortable here.” Two lux is 10 to 20 times the illuminance of a full moon. We can see each other clearly, and a nearby sign assures us that closed-circuit television (CCTV) is in operation for safety’s sake.

Light designer Simon Thorp uses a luxmeter to measure the intensity of light emitted by a street lamp in St. James’s Park, London.

Luigi Avantaggiato

Trafalgar Square captures the relationship between lighting and darkness that exists in almost every city, suburb, small town, and village around the world. The lighting here is a mishmash of old technologies and new, of shadow and glare, the ornamental gas lamps fronting the National Gallery all but washed out by the LEDs inside modern versions of traditional “brass and glass” fixtures a few meters away—21st-century technology housed in 19th-century designs.

The ugly truth of artificial lighting today is that in parts of London, as in many cities around the world, lighting levels are excessive, with unshielded illumination blasting in all directions. And the irony is that too much light invites danger: It creates shadows, impedes our vision, and gives the illusion, without the reality, of safety. Thorp notes that modern CCTV cameras are “pretty great” even at low-light levels, while harsh light makes it hard for both human eyes and digital sensors to see.

“The more bad light we add, the more bad light we think we need,” says Thorp. “We can’t see because of the light we’ve added. And it makes areas that were perfectly okay seem darker.”

Among the costs of this excess, the most alarming may be its toll on human health (and that of other animals) by disrupting circadian rhythms, impeding the production of melatonin, and contributing to sleep disorders that are tied to every major modern disease. New research shows that increased exposure to blue light from LEDs is having “substantial biological impacts” such as suppression of the sleep hormone melatonin and an increased risk for obesity, certain cancers, and type 2 diabetes.

A panoramic view from the Eiffel Tower looks down on the illuminated Champ de Mars gardens [center] leading toward the École Militaire, with the tall silhouette of the Tour Montparnasse visible on the Paris skyline.

Luigi Avantaggiato

I have come to London and Paris—which led the way in the expansion of public street lighting in the 19th century—because they embody both the current enormity of the problem as well as a future certain to be lit by trillions of chips: controllable, tunable, and energy-efficient LEDs.

Living with artificial light at night

The standard justification for nighttime illumination is public safety. Lighting experts’ term for the phenomenon is “artificial light at night.” While people won’t often admit it, the desire for light at night seems to stem from a primal fear of the dark. Darkness is where the bad guys hide. And if dark is bad and light is good, then more light can only be better.

This assumption has guided our use of nighttime light for hundreds of years. And yet, high-lumen output doesn’t necessarily correlate to a reduction in crime, research has found. In other words, if we relied on the data as much as we do our primal anxieties and paused those anxieties long enough to learn how light and darkness interact, our nights would almost certainly be lighted differently—especially now that we have the extraordinary technology that is the light-emitting diode.

A 19th-century gas lamp [white square] manufactured by William Sugg & Co. next to a pedestrian path in Trafalgar Square, along with the architectural floodlighting on the neoclassical facade of the National Gallery, showcase the interplay between modern and historic lighting systems.

Luigi Avantaggiato

The first visible red light-emitting diode was invented by Nick Holonyak in 1962, but LED lighting technology took several decades to develop, before exploding in recent years. Just a decade ago, LED streetlamps were rare. By 2019, more than half of U.S. streetlights were LEDs, and that number is predicted to top 90 percent by 2030. Similar uptake has occurred around the world, even in developing countries, where inexpensive Chinese-made LED fixtures are increasingly common.

This rapid global migration to LEDs represents a shift in the fundamental physics of how we illuminate our world. From oil lamps and candles to gas lamps, early examples of artificial light at night relied on a burning wick, an incredibly inefficient way to create light. An incandescent bulb is effectively a heater that happens to produce light as a by-product, so it squanders nearly all of its energy as heat.

By contrast, LEDs use semiconductors to convert electricity into light. Through this process of electroluminescence, LEDs use up to 90 percent less energy than incandescent bulbs do, which has enabled municipalities to realize an immediate energy savings of 50 percent or more. This fact alone has fueled the technology’s worldwide adoption.

But LEDs aren’t just more efficient and less expensive. The use of solid-state technology gives the lights an extraordinary life-span, often measured in decades rather than years. This significantly lowers the maintenance costs, as city workers spend far fewer hours replacing broken or burned-out lights. Even more striking, by manipulating the properties of the semiconductor material, engineers can dictate the precise color and intensity of the output, something that gives LEDs incredible versatility. For a lighting designer like Thorp, LEDs offer countless possibilities.

Digital control for smarter lighting

Wandering from Trafalgar Square along the edge of St. James’s Park, Thorp and I find ourselves near Westminster Bridge, one of nine city bridges that in 2021 were part of the Illuminated River project, meant to make the Thames more beautiful at night. Each bridge now features a new LED lighting scheme that moves and changes color and intensity to create a coordinated work of art. But Thorp is frustrated that the project did nothing to correct the often glary lighting on the riverbanks. “Why don’t you pay the money to correct all of this bad lighting instead of adding new lighting?” he says. LED technology, he points out, has the potential to fix that problem.

The Illuminated River artwork for Blackfriars Bridge uses a color scheme that closely complements the red pillar supports that remain from the original Blackfriars Railway Bridge.

Luigi Avantaggiato

In fact, this may be the most meaningful potential of LEDs: the ability for a community to control when, where, at what levels, and in which colors its lights shine. A public space like Trafalgar Square could be lit more brightly during rush hour, then dimmed as the night progresses, the lights not only connected to one another but to the surrounding streetlights and commercial lights. Anywhere in the world, LED streetlights could be programmed to rise and fall in brightness depending on the time of night or time of year. They could even be turned off during bird migrations, to reduce the number of birds that are disoriented by the lights and ultimately killed in collisions with reflective and illuminated windows.

Up to now, LED public lighting has largely not been part of any comprehensive plan to curtail and control nighttime illumination. Most LED installations have simply replaced older, inefficient “dumb” electric lighting with newer “dumb” LEDs and thus made light pollution worse. The main reason? Because LED lighting is cheaper, we tend to use more of it—a literally shining example of the Jevons paradox. Even as awareness of light pollution grows, we aren’t yet taking advantage of LED technology’s full potential.

The good news is that we could start tonight. At DarkSky International, the world’s foremost organization fighting light pollution, CEO and executive director Ruskin Hartley tells me the organization has five principles for responsible outdoor lighting: It should be useful, targeted, low level, controlled, and warm-colored. “They’re enabled because of the capabilities of LEDs,” Hartley says.

The technology to control LEDs will be part of the solution. In the olden days of the analog era, a streetlight was either on or off. To change a lighting schedule, you had to physically rewire a circuit. Today, the Digital Addressable Lighting Interface (DALI) protocol turns each luminaire into part of a network, with its own digital address and a driver that reports to a central server. With DALI, the lighting is managed through software rather than physical switches.

Unfortunately, most LED streetlights have been deployed without this technology because it costs more. But Paul Drosihn, general manager of the DALI Alliance, says that using such controls makes the LEDs much easier to maintain. Before the new digital protocol, it took an average of nearly three visits to identify, diagnose, and repair a defective luminaire. “Now it’s one,” Drosihn says. “Saving the cost of sending two guys on a cherry picker to replace those [lights] is immeasurable. What I just described to you is pretty much all the utilities need to know.”

The intricate cast-iron understructure of Westminster Bridge glows in vibrant green and teal light as part of the Illuminated River public art project, a color palette selected to echo the green benches of the nearby House of Commons.

Luigi Avantaggiato

What’s more, DALI allows the tuning of the lights’ spectrum so that they become warmer and less disruptive as the night progresses. Digitally connected, full-spectrum luminaires allow cities to transform the nocturnal experience—saving money, increasing health and safety, and creating a warmer and more appealing atmosphere at night.

This digital intelligence is equally transformative for the “bleed lighting” that spills from building interiors, Drosihn says. “You don’t think of night lighting as coming from inside buildings, but it does,” he says. “Particularly in the States, you drive through any major city and all the lights are on, on every floor of every high-rise, even if no one is home.”

Already, the use of digital controls for interior lighting has become commonplace in some European cities, Drosihn says. By integrating DALI with occupancy sensors and building-management systems that monitor HVAC, electrical systems, and security networks, a skyscraper can become a dynamic participant in the urban environment—dropping a floor’s interior lights to zero the moment the last person leaves. As a result, electronic controls combined with LEDs can act like a dimmer switch for a city’s entire skyline.

White light blights the night

With all the possibilities from LED technology, why are our nights too often lit with harsh and clinical light, casting glare and creating shadows, disrupting human and ecological health, erasing the stars from our skies? The answer starts with the color of LEDs. At first glance, an LED streetlight looks like a collection of small white bulbs, but it’s not. To produce a light we perceive as white, most manufacturers coat a blue semiconductor core with a yellow phosphor material that absorbs a portion of that high-energy blue light. The problem is that this “white” light is still heavily blue, which is exactly the color no species has evolved to expect at night.

A historic three-lantern cast-iron street lamp along the pedestrian walkway of Westminster Bridge casts a glow against the night sky.

Luigi Avantaggiato

And because blue light is the second most energetic part of the visible spectrum (violet is the most), it doesn’t just illuminate our streets and invade our homes. Blue light also scatters in the atmosphere more easily than any other color, which helps to create the hazy, illuminated fog known as sky glow over every city of any size.

“Cooler” colored LEDs in the 4,000- to 6,500-kelvin range offer the most lumens at the lowest cost, so most early adopters installed these blue-rich white lights. The good news is that LED technology has continued to advance, and a growing number of communities are choosing warmer-colored streetlights that have less blue. (Phoenix, for example, converted 100,000 streetlamps to 2,700 K LEDs in 2020.) And, of course, light pollution isn’t just a result of LEDs. Older lighting technology also adds to the glare—bright white metal-halide lights, especially—and cities are loath to replace something that isn’t yet broken.

But our main failure isn’t a technical one. It’s that we have yet to revise our thinking about lighting at night. We use LED technology just as we did the old sources of light. As a result, we have largely offset the gains that were promised in terms of reducing energy consumption and carbon emissions by making light pollution worse, and have so far let an incredible opportunity go unrealized.

Can the City of Light do it right?

Across the Channel in Paris, the failure to realize the potential of LEDs feels even more palpable. Unlike London, which suffered heavily from German bombs, the lovely 19th-century Paris that Baron Haussmann created largely escaped destruction in World War II. To nearly 50 million annual tourists, the beautiful uniformity of the architecture is instantly recognizable. But the City of Light’s nocturnal atmosphere is also part of the draw, and extensive attention has been given to relighting its buildings and monuments. When I wander into the Cour Carrée in the Louvre, for example, I’m stunned by rows of amber LEDs that together create a warm glow along the palace facades. When I see the Eiffel Tower, first from a distance walking along the Seine and then up close, I find myself staring as I would at a campfire, the structure’s metalwork amber-lit with more than 336 high-pressure sodium bulbs.

The Eiffel Tower's signature golden illumination was inaugurated in 1985. The 336 high-pressure sodium projectors are installed directly inside the monument's structure.

Luigi Avantaggiato; Lighting designer for the golden lights of the Eiffel Tower: Pierre Bideau

Still, the city’s night lighting is far from perfect. With millions of residents, thousands of stores and restaurants, and 300,000 streetlights, the city overall is among the world’s brightest. Even at the base of the Tower, bright white LED lamps illuminate the African émigrés selling cheap berets and Tour de France trinkets. And the city has been replacing its old sodium streetlights with new LEDs, swapping the warm yellow tones for which the city has long been known for bright white lamps no one wants to look at.

Street vendors display souvenirs at the foot of the Eiffel Tower. Their merchandise is lit by harsh white LED systems, which starkly contrast with the warm golden sodium-vapor light illuminating the tower above.

Luigi Avantaggiato

Nonetheless, the potential is here. In 2019, France introduced a nationwide law to reduce levels of light pollution, setting rules about both public lighting (preventing light from being projected above the horizontal) and private lighting such as stores, which are required to turn off their exterior and shop window lights after 1 a.m. In addition, an increasing number of French communities dim or turn off municipal lights after midnight to save energy and reduce carbon emissions. Although light pollution worldwide continues to increase by nearly 10 percent per year, France has managed to reduce its overall level. Chloé Beaudet, a researcher at Université Paris-Saclay, documented local light-reduction measures and found people generally agreed with the notion of dimming or turning off the lights, mainly for energy savings and ecological concerns.

Researcher Chloé Beaudet looks toward the Notre-Dame cathedral from the nearby Pont de l’Archevêché (Archbishop’s Bridge).

Luigi Avantaggiato

“What I find is that people living in urban areas, they accept this kind of policy,” she tells me. “They’re like, okay, I don’t really use public space at night as a pedestrian, so what’s the point of having lights on?” For her, a key takeaway is that one lighting level does not fit all areas. “I think there is really a need for policy that is differentiated according to the neighborhood.”

The west facade of the Notre-Dame cathedral glows at night following its restoration, with architectural LED lighting illuminating the three monumental portals, rose window, and twin towers in a warm white light that preserves the medieval limestone details.

Luigi Avantaggiato

In another positive development, the country has been minimizing artificial light to create ecological corridors designed to protect nocturnal species such as birds, bats, and insects. These corridors are connected and dark, mitigating the disruption to the 30 percent of vertebrates and more than 60 percent of invertebrates that are nocturnal. Even for city dwellers, this trame noire (“dark infrastructure”) helps to raise awareness of why controlling light pollution is important for life on Earth. Nationwide laws to control light pollution, the ability to light different parts of a city differently, dark corridors to protect biodiversity—these are exactly the kind of changes made possible with LEDs.

The iconic I.M. Pei Pyramid glows softly at the center of the Cour Napoléon at the Louvre Museum, its warm LED illumination flowing through the geometric glass-and-metal structure with a symmetrical framing of the surrounding historic pavilions against the night sky.

Luigi Avantaggiato

That’s not all. Almost until 1920, astronomers at the Paris Observatory were still gazing at the Milky Way. That’s impossible nowadays, but it could happen again. Despite the bright white LED streetlights now lining so many Paris streets, networks of LEDs using controls could lower lighting levels enough each night, so that the Milky Way could once again be visible over the French capital. And in the process, Paris could become the City of Light in ways that would set an example for other parts of the world.

New lighting demands new thinking

“I think we should aspire to have cities that see the stars,” Simon Thorp says when I mention this view of Paris. “You just need everything to be coordinated.”

Nearing the end of our London walk, having turned from the river and back up toward the Strand, Thorp brings me down narrow Carting Lane behind the Savoy Hotel, to where a gas fixture tops a thick lamppost, an original from 1870. A small plaque reads, “The last remaining sewer gas destructor lamp in the city of Westminster.” Thorp explains that the thick pole hides a tube that allowed methane from the sewers to get burned off at the mantle. “An early example of renewable energy,” he jokes.

The fire-orange flame is pleasing to the eye. But even here, on a narrow lane with no vehicle traffic, in a touristy area of the city, the flame is overwhelmed by a nearby, unshielded LED security light. Thorp shakes his head. “It’s stunning that someone could put in a light like that and think, ‘Great, nice job.’”

Tourists gaze at the Webb Patent Sewer Gas Lamp, London’s last remaining Victorian sewer-ventilating street light, which illuminates the rain-slicked pavement of Carting Lane just off the Strand. Invented in the late 19th century by Joseph Webb to burn off methane from the subterranean network, the lamp operates 24 hours a day.

Luigi Avantaggiato

Here is the crux of contemporary artificial lighting at night. We know how to light well, and LEDs give us the ability to do so. But while our technology is 21st century, too often our thinking about light and darkness, safety and security, is stuck in the past. We could be doing so much more with this technology than we are. We could relight our nights in ways that would not only reduce energy and maintenance costs but also bring a slew of benefits, including healthier nights for humans, safer skies for nocturnal creatures, and a restoration of the stars.

In 2026, the tale of these two cities and their artificial light at night is that of a brilliant technology that we’ve engineered but haven’t yet learned to master. In short, we have yet to change the way we think about artificial light at night and to use it more thoughtfully and carefully—as we might, as one hopes we will.

With millions of residents, thousands of stores and restaurants, and 300,000 streetlights, the city overall is among the world’s brightest. Even at the base of the Tower, bright white LED lamps illuminate the African émigrés selling cheap berets and Tour de France trinkets. And the city has been replacing its old sodium streetlights with new LEDs, swapping the warm yellow tones for which the city has long been known for bright white lamps no one wants to look at.Street vendors display souvenirs at the foot of the Eiffel Tower. Their merchandise is lit by harsh white LED systems, which starkly contrast with the warm golden sodium-vapor light illuminating the tower above.Luigi AvantaggiatoNonetheless, the potential is here. In 2019, France introduced a nationwide law to reduce levels of light pollution, setting rules about both public lighting (preventing light from being projected above the horizontal) and private lighting such as stores, which are required to turn off their exterior and shop window lights after 1 a.m. In addition, an increasing number of French communities dim or turn off municipal lights after midnight to save energy and reduce carbon emissions. Although light pollution worldwide continues to increase by nearly 10 percent per year, France has managed to reduce its overall level. Chloé Beaudet, a researcher at Université Paris-Saclay, documented local light-reduction measures and found people generally agreed with the notion of dimming or turning off the lights, mainly for energy savings and ecological concerns.Researcher Chloé Beaudet looks toward the Notre-Dame cathedral from the nearby Pont de l’Archevêché (Archbishop’s Bridge).Luigi Avantaggiato“What I find is that people living in urban areas, they accept this kind of policy,” she tells me. “They’re like, okay, I don’t really use public space at night as a pedestrian, so what’s the point of having lights on?” For her, a key takeaway is that one lighting level does not fit all areas. “I think there is really a need for policy that is differentiated according to the neighborhood.”The west facade of the Notre-Dame cathedral glows at night following its restoration, with architectural LED lighting illuminating the three monumental portals, rose window, and twin towers in a warm white light that preserves the medieval limestone details.Luigi AvantaggiatoIn another positive development, the country has been minimizing artificial light to create ecological corridors designed to protect nocturnal species such as birds, bats, and insects. These corridors are connected and dark, mitigating the disruption to the 30 percent of vertebrates and more than 60 percent of invertebrates that are nocturnal. Even for city dwellers, this trame noire (“dark infrastructure”) helps to raise awareness of why controlling light pollution is important for life on Earth. Nationwide laws to control light pollution, the ability to light different parts of a city differently, dark corridors to protect biodiversity—these are exactly the kind of changes made possible with LEDs.The iconic I.M. Pei Pyramid glows softly at the center of the Cour Napoléon at the Louvre Museum, its warm LED illumination flowing through the geometric glass-and-metal structure with a symmetrical framing of the surrounding historic pavilions against the night sky.Luigi AvantaggiatoThat’s not all. Almost until 1920, astronomers at the Paris Observatory were still gazing at the Milky Way. That’s impossible nowadays, but it could happen again. Despite the bright white LED streetlights now lining so many Paris streets, networks of LEDs using controls could lower lighting levels enough each night, so that the Milky Way could once again be visible over the French capital. And in the process, Paris could become the City of Light in ways that would set an example for other parts of the world.New lighting demands new thinking“I think we should aspire to have cities that see the stars,” Simon Thorp says when I mention this view of Paris. “You just need everything to be coordinated.”Nearing the end of our London walk, having turned from the river and back up toward the Strand, Thorp brings me down narrow Carting Lane behind the Savoy Hotel, to where a gas fixture tops a thick lamppost, an original from 1870. A small plaque reads, “The last remaining sewer gas destructor lamp in the city of Westminster.” Thorp explains that the thick pole hides a tube that allowed methane from the sewers to get burned off at the mantle. “An early example of renewable energy,” he jokes.The fire-orange flame is pleasing to the eye. But even here, on a narrow lane with no vehicle traffic, in a touristy area of the city, the flame is overwhelmed by a nearby, unshielded LED security light. Thorp shakes his head. “It’s stunning that someone could put in a light like that and think, ‘Great, nice job.’”Tourists gaze at the Webb Patent Sewer Gas Lamp, London’s last remaining Victorian sewer-ventilating street light, which illuminates the rain-slicked pavement of Carting Lane just off the Strand. Invented in the late 19th century by Joseph Webb to burn off methane from the subterranean network, the lamp operates 24 hours a day.Luigi AvantaggiatoHere is the crux of contemporary artificial lighting at night. We know how to light well, and LEDs give us the ability to do so. But while our technology is 21st century, too often our thinking about light and darkness, safety and security, is stuck in the past. We could be doing so much more with this technology than we are. We could relight our nights in ways that would not only reduce energy and maintenance costs but also bring a slew of benefits, including healthier nights for humans, safer skies for nocturnal creatures, and a restoration of the stars.In 2026, the tale of these two cities and their artificial light at night is that of a brilliant technology that we’ve engineered but haven’t yet learned to master. In short, we have yet to change the way we think about artificial light at night and to use it more thoughtfully and carefully—as we might, as one hopes we will.

blocks.html_en: In the chill of a London spring night, under overcast skies, iconic Trafalgar Square opens around me. Admiral Nelson rises on his pedestal, the National Gallery rests behind, the church of St Martin-in-the-Fields sits nearby. From the 13th century, the site served as the Royal Mews for hawks and then horses. By 1844, it was a public space at the heart of one of the biggest cities in the world.

sentences.zh: 在伦敦寒冷的春夜，阴沉的天空下，标志性的特拉法加广场在我周围展开。

sentences.en: Admiral Nelson rises on his pedestal, the National Gallery rests behind, the church of St Martin-in-the-Fields sits nearby.
sentences.zh: 纳尔逊海军上将站在他的基座上，国家美术馆位于后面，圣马丁教堂位于附近。

sentences.en: From the 13th century, the site served as the Royal Mews for hawks and then horses.

sentences.en: By 1844, it was a public space at the heart of one of the biggest cities in the world.
sentences.zh: 到 1844 年，它已成为世界上最大城市之一中心的公共空间。

blocks.html_en: Despite the square’s presence through that grand sweep of history, it’s not why I’m here. My interest is far more specific: I want to find out what happens to spaces like this when artificial light, specifically from light-emitting diodes (LEDs), intrudes. My companion tonight is Simon Thorp, a local lighting designer, who crouches in the shadows near Nelson’s spire, light meter in hand. “Two lux,” he reports, “and it’s very comfortable here.” Two lux is 10 to 20 times the illuminance of a full moon. We can see each other clearly, and a nearby sign assures us that closed-circuit television (CCTV) is in operation for safety’s sake.

sentences.en: Despite the square’s presence through that grand sweep of history, it’s not why I’m here.
sentences.zh: 尽管广场在漫长的历史中一直存在，但这并不是我来这里的原因。

sentences.en: My interest is far more specific: I want to find out what happens to spaces like this when artificial light, specifically from light-emitting diodes (LEDs), intrudes.
sentences.zh: 我的兴趣更为具体：我想了解当人造光（特别是发光二极管 (LED) 的人造光）侵入时，这样的空间会发生什么情况。

blocks.html_en: Trafalgar Square captures the relationship between lighting and darkness that exists in almost every city, suburb, small town, and village around the world. The lighting here is a mishmash of old technologies and new, of shadow and glare, the ornamental gas lamps fronting the National Gallery all but washed out by the LEDs inside modern versions of traditional “brass and glass” fixtures a few meters away—21st-century technology housed in 19th-century designs.

sentences.en: Trafalgar Square captures the relationship between lighting and darkness that exists in almost every city, suburb, small town, and village around the world.
sentences.zh: 特拉法加广场捕捉了世界上几乎每个城市、郊区、小镇和村庄中存在的光明与黑暗之间的关系。

sentences.en: The lighting here is a mishmash of old technologies and new, of shadow and glare, the ornamental gas lamps fronting the National Gallery all but washed out by the LEDs inside modern versions of traditional “brass and glass” fixtures a few meters away—21st-century technology housed in 19th-century designs.
sentences.zh: 这里的照明是旧技术和新技术、阴影和眩光的混合体，国家美术馆前面的装饰性煤气灯几乎被几米外传统“黄铜和玻璃”灯具的现代版本的 LED 冲淡了——21 世纪的技术融入了 19 世纪的设计。

blocks.html_en: The ugly truth of artificial lighting today is that in parts of London, as in many cities around the world, lighting levels are excessive, with unshielded illumination blasting in all directions. And the irony is that too much light invites danger: It creates shadows, impedes our vision, and gives the illusion, without the reality, of safety. Thorp notes that modern CCTV cameras are “pretty great” even at low-light levels, while harsh light makes it hard for both human eyes and digital sensors to see.

sentences.en: The ugly truth of artificial lighting today is that in parts of London, as in many cities around the world, lighting levels are excessive, with unshielded illumination blasting in all directions.
sentences.zh: 当今人工照明的丑陋事实是，在伦敦的部分地区，就像在世界上许多城市一​​样，照明水平过高，各个方向都有无遮蔽的照明。

sentences.en: And the irony is that too much light invites danger: It creates shadows, impedes our vision, and gives the illusion, without the reality, of safety.
sentences.zh: 具有讽刺意味的是，太多的光线会带来危险：它会产生阴影，阻碍我们的视野，并给人一种安全的错觉，而不是现实。

sentences.en: Thorp notes that modern CCTV cameras are “pretty great” even at low-light levels, while harsh light makes it hard for both human eyes and digital sensors to see.
sentences.zh: 索普指出，即使在弱光条件下，现代闭路电视摄像机也“相当出色”，而刺眼的光线使人眼和数字传感器都难以看到。

blocks.html_en: “The more bad light we add, the more bad light we think we need,” says Thorp. “We can’t see because of the light we’ve added. And it makes areas that were perfectly okay seem darker.”

sentences.en: “The more bad light we add, the more bad light we think we need,” says Thorp.
sentences.zh: “我们添加的坏光越多，我们认为我们需要的坏光就越多，”索普说。

sentences.en: “We can’t see because of the light we’ve added.
sentences.zh: “由于我们添加了光线，我们看不到。

sentences.en: And it makes areas that were perfectly okay seem darker.”
sentences.zh: 而且它会让本来完全正常的区域看起来更暗。”

blocks.html_en: Among the costs of this excess, the most alarming may be its toll on human health (and that of other animals) by disrupting circadian rhythms, impeding the production of melatonin, and contributing to sleep disorders that are tied to every major modern disease. New research shows that increased exposure to blue light from LEDs is having “substantial biological impacts” such as suppression of the sleep hormone melatonin and an increased risk for obesity, certain cancers, and type 2 diabetes.

sentences.en: Among the costs of this excess, the most alarming may be its toll on human health (and that of other animals) by disrupting circadian rhythms, impeding the production of melatonin, and contributing to sleep disorders that are tied to every major modern disease.
sentences.zh: 在这种过量的代价中，最令人震惊的可能是它通过扰乱昼夜节律、阻碍褪黑激素的产生以及导致与每种主要现代疾病相关的睡眠障碍而对人类健康（以及其他动物）造成的损害。

sentences.en: New research shows that increased exposure to blue light from LEDs is having “substantial biological impacts” such as suppression of the sleep hormone melatonin and an increased risk for obesity, certain cancers, and type 2 diabetes.
sentences.zh: 新研究表明，更多地接触 LED 发出的蓝光会产生“重大生物学影响”，例如抑制睡眠激素褪黑激素以及增加患肥胖症、某些癌症和 2 型糖尿病的风险。

blocks.html_en: I have come to London and Paris—which led the way in the expansion of public street lighting in the 19th century—because they embody both the current enormity of the problem as well as a future certain to be lit by trillions of chips: controllable, tunable, and energy-efficient LEDs.

sentences.en: I have come to London and Paris—which led the way in the expansion of public street lighting in the 19th century—because they embody both the current enormity of the problem as well as a future certain to be lit by trillions of chips: controllable, tunable, and energy-efficient LEDs.
sentences.zh: 我来到伦敦和巴黎——这两个国家在 19 世纪公共街道照明的扩张中处于领先地位——因为它们既体现了当前问题的严重性，也体现了未来肯定会由数万亿个芯片照亮：可控、可调且节能的 LED。

blocks.html_en: Living with artificial light at night

sentences.en: Living with artificial light at night
sentences.zh: 晚上生活在人造光下

blocks.html_en: The standard justification for nighttime illumination is public safety. Lighting experts’ term for the phenomenon is “artificial light at night.” While people won’t often admit it, the desire for light at night seems to stem from a primal fear of the dark. Darkness is where the bad guys hide. And if dark is bad and light is good, then more light can only be better.

sentences.en: The standard justification for nighttime illumination is public safety.
sentences.zh: 夜间照明的标准理由是公共安全。

sentences.en: Lighting experts’ term for the phenomenon is “artificial light at night.”
sentences.zh: 照明专家对这种现象的术语是“夜间人造光”。

sentences.en: While people won’t often admit it, the desire for light at night seems to stem from a primal fear of the dark.
sentences.zh: 尽管人们常常不承认这一点，但对夜间光明的渴望似乎源于对黑暗的原始恐惧。

sentences.en: Darkness is where the bad guys hide.
sentences.zh: 黑暗是坏人藏身的地方。

sentences.en: And if dark is bad and light is good, then more light can only be better.
sentences.zh: 如果黑暗不好而光好，那么更多的光只会更好。

blocks.html_en: This assumption has guided our use of nighttime light for hundreds of years. And yet, high-lumen output doesn’t necessarily correlate to a reduction in crime, research has found. In other words, if we relied on the data as much as we do our primal anxieties and paused those anxieties long enough to learn how light and darkness interact, our nights would almost certainly be lighted differently—especially now that we have the extraordinary technology that is the light-emitting diode.

sentences.en: This assumption has guided our use of nighttime light for hundreds of years.
sentences.zh: 数百年来，这一假设一直指导着我们对夜间灯光的使用。

sentences.en: And yet, high-lumen output doesn’t necessarily correlate to a reduction in crime, research has found.
sentences.zh: 然而，研究发现，高流明输出并不一定与犯罪率减少相关。

sentences.en: In other words, if we relied on the data as much as we do our primal anxieties and paused those anxieties long enough to learn how light and darkness interact, our nights would almost certainly be lighted differently—especially now that we have the extraordinary technology that is the light-emitting diode.
sentences.zh: 换句话说，如果我们像依赖原始焦虑一样依赖数据，并将这些焦虑暂停足够长的时间来了解光明和黑暗如何相互作用，那么我们的夜晚几乎肯定会被不同地照亮——尤其是现在我们拥有了非凡的技术，即发光二极管。

blocks.html_en: A 19th-century gas lamp [white square] manufactured by William Sugg & Co. next to a pedestrian path in Trafalgar Square, along with the architectural floodlighting on the neoclassical facade of the National Gallery, showcase the interplay between modern and historic lighting systems.

sentences.en: A 19th-century gas lamp [white square] manufactured by William Sugg & Co.
sentences.zh: William Sugg & Co. 制造的 19 世纪煤气灯 [白色方块]

sentences.en: next to a pedestrian path in Trafalgar Square, along with the architectural floodlighting on the neoclassical facade of the National Gallery, showcase the interplay between modern and historic lighting systems.
sentences.zh: 特拉法加广场的人行道旁边，以及国家美术馆新古典主义立面上的建筑泛光照明，展示了现代和历史照明系统之间的相互作用。

blocks.html_en: Luigi Avantaggiato

sentences.en: Luigi Avantaggiato
sentences.zh: 路易吉·阿万塔吉亚托

blocks.html_en: The first visible red light-emitting diode was invented by Nick Holonyak in 1962, but LED lighting technology took several decades to develop, before exploding in recent years. Just a decade ago, LED streetlamps were rare. By 2019, more than half of U.S. streetlights were LEDs, and that number is predicted to top 90 percent by 2030. Similar uptake has occurred around the world, even in developing countries, where inexpensive Chinese-made LED fixtures are increasingly common.

sentences.en: The first visible red light-emitting diode was invented by Nick Holonyak in 1962, but LED lighting technology took several decades to develop, before exploding in recent years.
sentences.zh: 第一个可见红色发光二极管由尼克·霍洛尼亚克 (Nick Holonyak) 于 1962 年发明，但 LED 照明技术花了几十年的时间才发展起来，直到近年来才出现爆炸式增长。

sentences.en: Just a decade ago, LED streetlamps were rare.
sentences.zh: 就在十年前，LED 路灯还很少见。

sentences.en: By 2019, more than half of U.S. streetlights were LEDs, and that number is predicted to top 90 percent by 2030.
sentences.zh: 到 2019 年，美国一半以上的路灯都是 LED，预计到 2030 年这一数字将达到 90%。

sentences.en: Similar uptake has occurred around the world, even in developing countries, where inexpensive Chinese-made LED fixtures are increasingly common.
sentences.zh: 世界各地都出现了类似的情况，甚至在发展中国家，廉价的中国制造的 LED 灯具也越来越普遍。

blocks.html_en: This rapid global migration to LEDs represents a shift in the fundamental physics of how we illuminate our world. From oil lamps and candles to gas lamps, early examples of artificial light at night relied on a burning wick, an incredibly inefficient way to create light. An incandescent bulb is effectively a heater that happens to produce light as a by-product, so it squanders nearly all of its energy as heat.

sentences.en: This rapid global migration to LEDs represents a shift in the fundamental physics of how we illuminate our world.
sentences.zh: 全球范围内向 LED 的快速迁移代表了我们照亮世界的基本物理原理的转变。

sentences.en: From oil lamps and candles to gas lamps, early examples of artificial light at night relied on a burning wick, an incredibly inefficient way to create light.
sentences.zh: 从油灯、蜡烛到煤气灯，早期的夜间人造光依赖于燃烧的灯芯，这是一种极其低效的照明方式。

sentences.en: An incandescent bulb is effectively a heater that happens to produce light as a by-product, so it squanders nearly all of its energy as heat.
sentences.zh: 白炽灯泡实际上是一种加热器，它会产生光作为副产品，因此它几乎将所有能量都以热量的形式浪费掉了。

blocks.html_en: By contrast, LEDs use semiconductors to convert electricity into light. Through this process of electroluminescence, LEDs use up to 90 percent less energy than incandescent bulbs do, which has enabled municipalities to realize an immediate energy savings of 50 percent or more. This fact alone has fueled the technology’s worldwide adoption.

sentences.en: By contrast, LEDs use semiconductors to convert electricity into light.
sentences.zh: 相比之下，LED 使用半导体将电能转化为光。

sentences.en: Through this process of electroluminescence, LEDs use up to 90 percent less energy than incandescent bulbs do, which has enabled municipalities to realize an immediate energy savings of 50 percent or more.
sentences.zh: 通过这种电致发光过程，LED 的能耗比白炽灯泡减少 90%，这使得市政当局能够立即实现 50% 或更多的节能。

sentences.en: This fact alone has fueled the technology’s worldwide adoption.
sentences.zh: 仅这一事实就推动了该技术在全球范围内的采用。

blocks.html_en: But LEDs aren’t just more efficient and less expensive. The use of solid-state technology gives the lights an extraordinary life-span, often measured in decades rather than years. This significantly lowers the maintenance costs, as city workers spend far fewer hours replacing broken or burned-out lights. Even more striking, by manipulating the properties of the semiconductor material, engineers can dictate the precise color and intensity of the output, something that gives LEDs incredible versatility. For a lighting designer like Thorp, LEDs offer countless possibilities.

sentences.en: But LEDs aren’t just more efficient and less expensive.
sentences.zh: 但 LED 不仅效率更高、成本更低。

sentences.en: The use of solid-state technology gives the lights an extraordinary life-span, often measured in decades rather than years.
sentences.zh: 固态技术的使用使灯具有非凡的使用寿命，通常以几十年而不是几年来衡量。

sentences.en: This significantly lowers the maintenance costs, as city workers spend far fewer hours replacing broken or burned-out lights.
sentences.zh: 这大大降低了维护成本，因为城市工作人员更换损坏或烧坏的灯的时间要少得多。

sentences.en: Even more striking, by manipulating the properties of the semiconductor material, engineers can dictate the precise color and intensity of the output, something that gives LEDs incredible versatility.
sentences.zh: 更引人注目的是，通过操纵半导体材料的特性，工程师可以指定输出的精确颜色和强度，这为 LED 提供了令人难以置信的多功能性。

sentences.en: For a lighting designer like Thorp, LEDs offer countless possibilities.
sentences.zh: 对于像 Thorp 这样的照明设计师来说，LED 提供了无数的可能性。

blocks.html_en: Wandering from Trafalgar Square along the edge of St. James’s Park, Thorp and I find ourselves near Westminster Bridge, one of nine city bridges that in 2021 were part of the Illuminated River project, meant to make the Thames more beautiful at night. Each bridge now features a new LED lighting scheme that moves and changes color and intensity to create a coordinated work of art. But Thorp is frustrated that the project did nothing to correct the often glary lighting on the riverbanks. “Why don’t you pay the money to correct all of this bad lighting instead of adding new lighting?” he says. LED technology, he points out, has the potential to fix that problem.

blocks.html_en: In fact, this may be the most meaningful potential of LEDs: the ability for a community to control when, where, at what levels, and in which colors its lights shine. A public space like Trafalgar Square could be lit more brightly during rush hour, then dimmed as the night progresses, the lights not only connected to one another but to the surrounding streetlights and commercial lights. Anywhere in the world, LED streetlights could be programmed to rise and fall in brightness depending on the time of night or time of year. They could even be turned off during bird migrations, to reduce the number of birds that are disoriented by the lights and ultimately killed in collisions with reflective and illuminated windows.

blocks.html_en: Up to now, LED public lighting has largely not been part of any comprehensive plan to curtail and control nighttime illumination. Most LED installations have simply replaced older, inefficient “dumb” electric lighting with newer “dumb” LEDs and thus made light pollution worse. The main reason? Because LED lighting is cheaper, we tend to use more of it—a literally shining example of the Jevons paradox. Even as awareness of light pollution grows, we aren’t yet taking advantage of LED technology’s full potential.

blocks.html_en: The good news is that we could start tonight. At DarkSky International, the world’s foremost organization fighting light pollution, CEO and executive director Ruskin Hartley tells me the organization has five principles for responsible outdoor lighting: It should be useful, targeted, low level, controlled, and warm-colored. “They’re enabled because of the capabilities of LEDs,” Hartley says.

blocks.html_en: The technology to control LEDs will be part of the solution. In the olden days of the analog era, a streetlight was either on or off. To change a lighting schedule, you had to physically rewire a circuit. Today, the Digital Addressable Lighting Interface (DALI) protocol turns each luminaire into part of a network, with its own digital address and a driver that reports to a central server. With DALI, the lighting is managed through software rather than physical switches.

blocks.html_en: Unfortunately, most LED streetlights have been deployed without this technology because it costs more. But Paul Drosihn, general manager of the DALI Alliance, says that using such controls makes the LEDs much easier to maintain. Before the new digital protocol, it took an average of nearly three visits to identify, diagnose, and repair a defective luminaire. “Now it’s one,” Drosihn says. “Saving the cost of sending two guys on a cherry picker to replace those [lights] is immeasurable. What I just described to you is pretty much all the utilities need to know.”

Digitally connected, full-spectrum luminaires allow cities to transform the nocturnal experience—saving money, increasing health and safety, and creating a warmer and more appealing atmosphere at night.

blocks.html_en: Already, the use of digital controls for interior lighting has become commonplace in some European cities, Drosihn says. By integrating DALI with occupancy sensors and building-management systems that monitor HVAC, electrical systems, and security networks, a skyscraper can become a dynamic participant in the urban environment—dropping a floor’s interior lights to zero the moment the last person leaves. As a result, electronic controls combined with LEDs can act like a dimmer switch for a city’s entire skyline.

blocks.html_en: White light blights the night

sentences.en: White light blights the night

blocks.html_en: With all the possibilities from LED technology, why are our nights too often lit with harsh and clinical light, casting glare and creating shadows, disrupting human and ecological health, erasing the stars from our skies? The answer starts with the color of LEDs. At first glance, an LED streetlight looks like a collection of small white bulbs, but it’s not. To produce a light we perceive as white, most manufacturers coat a blue semiconductor core with a yellow phosphor material that absorbs a portion of that high-energy blue light. The problem is that this “white” light is still heavily blue, which is exactly the color no species has evolved to expect at night.

blocks.html_en: And because blue light is the second most energetic part of the visible spectrum (violet is the most), it doesn’t just illuminate our streets and invade our homes. Blue light also scatters in the atmosphere more easily than any other color, which helps to create the hazy, illuminated fog known as sky glow over every city of any size.

sentences.en: Blue light also scatters in the atmosphere more easily than any other color, which helps to create the hazy, illuminated fog known as sky glow over every city of any size.
sentences.zh: 蓝光比任何其他颜色更容易在大气中散射，这有助于在任何规模的每个城市上空形成朦胧、明亮的雾气，称为天空辉光。

blocks.html_en: “Cooler” colored LEDs in the 4,000- to 6,500-kelvin range offer the most lumens at the lowest cost, so most early adopters installed these blue-rich white lights. The good news is that LED technology has continued to advance, and a growing number of communities are choosing warmer-colored streetlights that have less blue. (Phoenix, for example, converted 100,000 streetlamps to 2,700 K LEDs in 2020.) And, of course, light pollution isn’t just a result of LEDs. Older lighting technology also adds to the glare—bright white metal-halide lights, especially—and cities are loath to replace something that isn’t yet broken.

blocks.html_en: But our main failure isn’t a technical one. It’s that we have yet to revise our thinking about lighting at night. We use LED technology just as we did the old sources of light. As a result, we have largely offset the gains that were promised in terms of reducing energy consumption and carbon emissions by making light pollution worse, and have so far let an incredible opportunity go unrealized.

blocks.html_en: Can the City of Light do it right?

sentences.en: Can the City of Light do it right?
sentences.zh: 光之城能做对吗？

blocks.html_en: Across the Channel in Paris, the failure to realize the potential of LEDs feels even more palpable. Unlike London, which suffered heavily from German bombs, the lovely 19th-century Paris that Baron Haussmann created largely escaped destruction in World War II. To nearly 50 million annual tourists, the beautiful uniformity of the architecture is instantly recognizable. But the City of Light’s nocturnal atmosphere is also part of the draw, and extensive attention has been given to relighting its buildings and monuments. When I wander into the Cour Carrée in the Louvre, for example, I’m stunned by rows of amber LEDs that together create a warm glow along the palace facades. When I see the Eiffel Tower, first from a distance walking along the Seine and then up close, I find myself staring as I would at a campfire, the structure’s metalwork amber-lit with more than 336 high-pressure sodium bulbs.

blocks.html_en: Still, the city’s night lighting is far from perfect. With millions of residents, thousands of stores and restaurants, and 300,000 streetlights, the city overall is among the world’s brightest. Even at the base of the Tower, bright white LED lamps illuminate the African émigrés selling cheap berets and Tour de France trinkets. And the city has been replacing its old sodium streetlights with new LEDs, swapping the warm yellow tones for which the city has long been known for bright white lamps no one wants to look at.

blocks.html_en: Nonetheless, the potential is here. In 2019, France introduced a nationwide law to reduce levels of light pollution, setting rules about both public lighting (preventing light from being projected above the horizontal) and private lighting such as stores, which are required to turn off their exterior and shop window lights after 1 a.m. In addition, an increasing number of French communities dim or turn off municipal lights after midnight to save energy and reduce carbon emissions. Although light pollution worldwide continues to increase by nearly 10 percent per year, France has managed to reduce its overall level. Chloé Beaudet, a researcher at Université Paris-Saclay, documented local light-reduction measures and found people generally agreed with the notion of dimming or turning off the lights, mainly for energy savings and ecological concerns.

sentences.en: Nonetheless, the potential is here.
sentences.zh: 尽管如此，潜力就在这里。

sentences.en: In 2019, France introduced a nationwide law to reduce levels of light pollution, setting rules about both public lighting (preventing light from being projected above the horizontal) and private lighting such as stores, which are required to turn off their exterior and shop window lights after 1 a.m.
sentences.zh: 2019年，法国出台了一项全国性法律，旨在降低光污染水平，对公共照明（防止光线投射到水平线以上）和商店等私人照明制定了规则，要求商店在凌晨1点后关闭外部和商店橱窗的灯光。

sentences.en: In addition, an increasing number of French communities dim or turn off municipal lights after midnight to save energy and reduce carbon emissions.
sentences.zh: 此外，越来越多的法国社区在午夜后调暗或关闭市政灯光，以节省能源并减少碳排放。

sentences.en: Although light pollution worldwide continues to increase by nearly 10 percent per year, France has managed to reduce its overall level.
sentences.zh: 尽管全球光污染继续以每年近 10% 的速度增加，但法国已成功降低其总体水平。

sentences.en: Chloé Beaudet, a researcher at Université Paris-Saclay, documented local light-reduction measures and found people generally agreed with the notion of dimming or turning off the lights, mainly for energy savings and ecological concerns.
sentences.zh: 巴黎萨克雷大学研究员 Chloé Beaudet 记录了当地的减光措施，发现人们普遍同意调暗或关灯的想法，主要是出于节能和生态考虑。

blocks.html_en: “What I find is that people living in urban areas, they accept this kind of policy,” she tells me. “They’re like, okay, I don’t really use public space at night as a pedestrian, so what’s the point of having lights on?” For her, a key takeaway is that one lighting level does not fit all areas. “I think there is really a need for policy that is differentiated according to the neighborhood.”

sentences.zh: “我发现生活在城市地区的人们接受这种政策，”她告诉我。

sentences.en: “They’re like, okay, I don’t really use public space at night as a pedestrian, so what’s the point of having lights on?”
sentences.zh: “他们说，好吧，我晚上作为行人并不真正使用公共空间，所以开灯有什么意义呢？”

sentences.en: For her, a key takeaway is that one lighting level does not fit all areas.
sentences.zh: 对她来说，一个重要的收获是一种照明水平并不适合所有区域。

sentences.en: “I think there is really a need for policy that is differentiated according to the neighborhood.”
sentences.zh: “我认为确实需要根据社区制定差异化的政策。”

blocks.html_en: In another positive development, the country has been minimizing artificial light to create ecological corridors designed to protect nocturnal species such as birds, bats, and insects. These corridors are connected and dark, mitigating the disruption to the 30 percent of vertebrates and more than 60 percent of invertebrates that are nocturnal. Even for city dwellers, this trame noire (“dark infrastructure”) helps to raise awareness of why controlling light pollution is important for life on Earth. Nationwide laws to control light pollution, the ability to light different parts of a city differently, dark corridors to protect biodiversity—these are exactly the kind of changes made possible with LEDs.

sentences.en: In another positive development, the country has been minimizing artificial light to create ecological corridors designed to protect nocturnal species such as birds, bats, and insects.

blocks.html_en: That’s not all. Almost until 1920, astronomers at the Paris Observatory were still gazing at the Milky Way. That’s impossible nowadays, but it could happen again. Despite the bright white LED streetlights now lining so many Paris streets, networks of LEDs using controls could lower lighting levels enough each night, so that the Milky Way could once again be visible over the French capital. And in the process, Paris could become the City of Light in ways that would set an example for other parts of the world.

blocks.html_en: Nearing the end of our London walk, having turned from the river and back up toward the Strand, Thorp brings me down narrow Carting Lane behind the Savoy Hotel, to where a gas fixture tops a thick lamppost, an original from 1870. A small plaque reads, “The last remaining sewer gas destructor lamp in the city of Westminster.” Thorp explains that the thick pole hides a tube that allowed methane from the sewers to get burned off at the mantle. “An early example of renewable energy,” he jokes.

blocks.html_en: Here is the crux of contemporary artificial lighting at night. We know how to light well, and LEDs give us the ability to do so. But while our technology is 21st century, too often our thinking about light and darkness, safety and security, is stuck in the past. We could be doing so much more with this technology than we are. We could relight our nights in ways that would not only reduce energy and maintenance costs but also bring a slew of benefits, including healthier nights for humans, safer skies for nocturnal creatures, and a restoration of the stars.

sentences.en: Here is the crux of contemporary artificial lighting at night.
sentences.zh: 这就是当代夜间人工照明的症结所在。

sentences.en: We know how to light well, and LEDs give us the ability to do so.
sentences.zh: 我们知道如何提供良好的照明，而 LED 使我们能够做到这一点。

sentences.en: But while our technology is 21st century, too often our thinking about light and darkness, safety and security, is stuck in the past.
sentences.zh: 然而，尽管我们的技术已经进入 21 世纪，但我们对光明与黑暗、安全与保障的思考却常常停留在过去。

sentences.en: We could be doing so much more with this technology than we are.
sentences.zh: 利用这项技术，我们可以做更多的事情。

sentences.en: We could relight our nights in ways that would not only reduce energy and maintenance costs but also bring a slew of benefits, including healthier nights for humans, safer skies for nocturnal creatures, and a restoration of the stars.
sentences.zh: 我们可以通过多种方式重新照亮我们的夜晚，不仅可以减少能源和维护成本，还可以带来一系列好处，包括人类更健康的夜晚、夜行动物更安全的天空以及星星的恢复。

blocks.html_en: In 2026, the tale of these two cities and their artificial light at night is that of a brilliant technology that we’ve engineered but haven’t yet learned to master. In short, we have yet to change the way we think about artificial light at night and to use it more thoughtfully and carefully—as we might, as one hopes we will.

sentences.zh: 2026 年，这两座城市及其夜间人造光的故事是我们设计但尚未学会掌握的一项出色技术的故事。

sentences.en: In short, we have yet to change the way we think about artificial light at night and to use it more thoughtfully and carefully—as we might, as one hopes we will.
sentences.zh: 简而言之，我们还没有改变对夜间人造光的看法，并更加深思熟虑和谨慎地使用它——正如我们所希望的那样。

content_html_zh:
沃克斯豪尔和九榆树摩天大楼群（左）以及米尔班克塔（右）的灯光反射的光线使泰晤士河泛着红光。

在伦敦寒冷的春夜，阴沉的天空下，标志性的特拉法加广场在我周围展开。 纳尔逊海军上将站在他的基座上，国家美术馆位于后面，圣马丁教堂位于附近。 从 13 世纪开始，这里就成为鹰的皇家马厩，后来又成为马的皇家马厩。 到 1844 年，它已成为世界上最大城市之一中心的公共空间。

尽管广场在漫长的历史中一直存在，但这并不是我来这里的原因。 我的兴趣更为具体：我想了解当人造光（特别是发光二极管 (LED) 的人造光）侵入时，这样的空间会发生什么情况。 今晚我的同伴是当地的灯光设计师西蒙·索普 (Simon Thorp)，他蹲在尼尔森尖塔附近的阴影中，手里拿着测光表。 “两勒克斯，”他报告道，“这里非常舒服。” 2勒克斯是满月照度的10到20倍。 我们可以清楚地看到对方，附近的标志向我们保证，为了安全起见，闭路电视（CCTV）正在运行。

灯光设计师西蒙·索普使用照度计测量伦敦圣詹姆斯公园路灯发出的光强度。 路易吉·阿万塔吉亚托

特拉法加广场捕捉了世界上几乎每个城市、郊区、小镇和村庄中存在的光明与黑暗之间的关系。 这里的照明是旧技术和新技术、阴影和眩光的混合体，国家美术馆前面的装饰性煤气灯几乎被几米外传统“黄铜和玻璃”灯具的现代版本的 LED 冲淡了——21 世纪的技术融入了 19 世纪的设计。

当今人工照明的丑陋事实是，在伦敦的部分地区，就像在世界上许多城市一​​样，照明水平过高，各个方向都有无遮蔽的照明。 具有讽刺意味的是，太多的光线会带来危险：它会产生阴影，阻碍我们的视野，并给人一种安全的错觉，而不是现实。 索普指出，即使在弱光条件下，现代闭路电视摄像机也“相当出色”，而刺眼的光线使人眼和数字传感器都难以看到。

“我们添加的坏光越多，我们认为我们需要的坏光就越多，”索普说。 “由于我们添加了光线，我们看不到。 而且它会让本来完全正常的区域看起来更暗。”

在这种过量的代价中，最令人震惊的可能是它通过扰乱昼夜节律、阻碍褪黑激素的产生以及导致与每种主要现代疾病相关的睡眠障碍而对人类健康（以及其他动物）造成的损害。 新研究表明，更多地接触 LED 发出的蓝光会产生“重大生物学影响”，例如抑制睡眠激素褪黑激素以及增加患肥胖症、某些癌症和 2 型糖尿病的风险。

从埃菲尔铁塔俯瞰全景，俯瞰灯火通明的战神花园（中），通向军事学院，蒙帕纳斯大厦高耸的轮廓在巴黎天际线上清晰可见。

路易吉·阿万塔吉亚托

我来到伦敦和巴黎——这两个国家在 19 世纪公共街道照明的扩张中处于领先地位——因为它们既体现了当前问题的严重性，也体现了未来肯定会由数万亿个芯片照亮：可控、可调且节能的 LED。

晚上生活在人造光下

夜间照明的标准理由是公共安全。 照明专家对这种现象的术语是“夜间人造光”。 尽管人们常常不承认这一点，但对夜间光明的渴望似乎源于对黑暗的原始恐惧。 黑暗是坏人藏身的地方。 如果黑暗不好而光好，那么更多的光只会更好。

数百年来，这一假设一直指导着我们对夜间灯光的使用。 然而，研究发现，高流明输出并不一定与犯罪率减少相关。 换句话说，如果我们像依赖原始焦虑一样依赖数据，并将这些焦虑暂停足够长的时间来了解光明和黑暗如何相互作用，那么我们的夜晚几乎肯定会被不同地照亮——尤其是现在我们拥有了非凡的技术，即发光二极管。

William Sugg & Co. 制造的 19 世纪煤气灯 [白色方块] 特拉法加广场的人行道旁边，以及国家美术馆新古典主义立面上的建筑泛光照明，展示了现代和历史照明系统之间的相互作用。

路易吉·阿万塔吉亚托

第一个可见红色发光二极管由尼克·霍洛尼亚克 (Nick Holonyak) 于 1962 年发明，但 LED 照明技术花了几十年的时间才发展起来，直到近年来才出现爆炸式增长。 就在十年前，LED 路灯还很少见。 到 2019 年，美国一半以上的路灯都是 LED，预计到 2030 年这一数字将达到 90%。 世界各地都出现了类似的情况，甚至在发展中国家，廉价的中国制造的 LED 灯具也越来越普遍。

全球范围内向 LED 的快速迁移代表了我们照亮世界的基本物理原理的转变。 从油灯、蜡烛到煤气灯，早期的夜间人造光依赖于燃烧的灯芯，这是一种极其低效的照明方式。 白炽灯泡实际上是一种加热器，它会产生光作为副产品，因此它几乎将所有能量都以热量的形式浪费掉了。

相比之下，LED 使用半导体将电能转化为光。 通过这种电致发光过程，LED 的能耗比白炽灯泡减少 90%，这使得市政当局能够立即实现 50% 或更多的节能。 仅这一事实就推动了该技术在全球范围内的采用。

但 LED 不仅效率更高、成本更低。 固态技术的使用使灯具有非凡的使用寿命，通常以几十年而不是几年来衡量。 这大大降低了维护成本，因为城市工作人员更换损坏或烧坏的灯的时间要少得多。 更引人注目的是，通过操纵半导体材料的特性，工程师可以指定输出的精确颜色和强度，这为 LED 提供了令人难以置信的多功能性。 对于像 Thorp 这样的照明设计师来说，LED 提供了无数的可能性。

数字控制实现更智能的照明

索普和我从特拉法加广场沿着圣詹姆斯公园边缘漫步，发现自己靠近威斯敏斯特大桥，这是 2021 年灯光河项目一部分的九座城市桥梁之一，旨在让泰晤士河在夜晚变得更加美丽。 现在，每座桥都采用了新的 LED 照明方案，可以移动、改变颜色和强度，创造出协调一致的艺术作品。 但索普感到沮丧的是，该项目没有采取任何措施来纠正河岸上经常眩目的灯光。 “你为什么不花钱来纠正所有这些不好的照明，而不是添加新的照明呢？” 他说。 他指出，LED 技术有可能解决这个问题。

Blackfriars 桥的照明河流艺术品采用的配色方案与原 Blackfriars 铁路桥留下的红色支柱支撑紧密互补。

路易吉·阿万塔吉亚托

事实上，这可能是 LED 最有意义的潜力：社区能够控制何时、何地、以什么级别以及以何种颜色发光。 像特拉法加广场这样的公共空间可以在高峰时段照明得更亮，然后随着夜晚的推移而变暗，灯光不仅相互连接，而且与周围的路灯和商业灯相连。 在世界任何地方，LED 路灯都可以根据夜晚或一年中的时间来调节亮度的上升和下降。 它们甚至可以在鸟类迁徙期间关闭，以减少因灯光而迷失方向并最终在与反光和照明窗户碰撞时死亡的鸟类数量。

到目前为止，LED 公共照明基本上还没有成为任何减少和控制夜间照明的综合计划的一部分。 大多数 LED 装置只是用更新的“哑”LED 取代了旧的、低效的“哑”电照明，从而使光污染更加严重。 主要原因是什么？ 由于 LED 照明更便宜，我们倾向于更多地使用它——这就是杰文斯悖论的一个光辉例子。 尽管人们对光污染的认识不断增强，但我们尚未充分利用 LED 技术的潜力。

好消息是我们今晚就可以开始。 DarkSky International 是世界上最重要的抗击光污染组织，其首席执行官兼执行董事 Ruskin Hartley 告诉我，该组织对负责任的户外照明有五个原则：它应该是有用的、有针对性的、低水平的、受控的和暖色的。 “它们之所以能够实现，是因为 LED 的功能，”Hartley 说。

控制 LED 的技术将成为解决方案的一部分。 在模拟时代的过去，路灯要么打开，要么关闭。 要更改照明时间表，您必须对电路进行物理重新布线。 如今，数字可寻址照明接口 (DALI) 协议将每个灯具变成网络的一部分，拥有自己的数字地址和向中央服务器报告的驱动程序。 使用 DALI，照明是通过软件而不是物理开关进行管理的。

不幸的是，大多数 LED 路灯的部署都没有采用这项技术，因为它的成本更高。 但 DALI 联盟总经理 Paul Drosihn 表示，使用此类控制装置使 LED 更容易维护。 在采用新的数字协议之前，平均需要近三次访问才能识别、诊断和修复有缺陷的灯具。 “现在是一个了，”德罗辛说。 “派两个人乘坐樱桃采摘机来更换这些[灯]所节省的成本是无法估量的。 我刚才向您描述的几乎是公用事业公司需要了解的所有内容。”

作为照明河公共艺术项目的一部分，威斯敏斯特桥错综复杂的铸铁底层结构闪烁着充满活力的绿色和青色光芒，该项目的调色板旨在呼应附近下议院的绿色长凳。

路易吉·阿万塔吉亚托

更重要的是，DALI 可以调整灯光的光谱，使灯光随着夜晚的推移而变得更温暖且干扰更少。 数字连接的全光谱灯具使城市能够改变夜间体验——节省资金、提高健康和安全性，并在夜间营造更温暖、更有吸引力的氛围。

德罗西恩说，这种数字智能对于建筑内部溢出的“出血照明”同样具有变革性。 “你不会认为夜间照明来自建筑物内部，但事实确实如此，”他说。 “特别是在美国，你开车穿过任何一个大城市，每座高层建筑的每一层楼的所有灯都亮着，即使没人在家。”

德罗西恩说，在一些欧洲城市，室内照明数字控制的使用已经变得司空见惯。 通过将 DALI 与监控 HVAC、电气系统和安全网络的占用传感器和建筑管理系统集成，摩天大楼可以成为城市环境的动态参与者——在最后一个人离开时将楼层的内部灯光降至零。 因此，电子控制装置与 LED 相结合，就像城市整个天际线的调光开关一样。

白光照亮了夜晚

凭借 LED 技术的所有可能性，为什么我们的夜晚经常被刺眼的临床光线照亮，投射眩光并产生阴影，破坏人类和生态健康，使天空中的星星消失？ 答案要从 LED 的颜色开始。 乍一看，LED 路灯看起来像是白​​色小灯泡的集合，但事实并非如此。 为了产生我们认为是白色的光，大多数制造商在蓝色半导体核心上涂上黄色磷光体材料，该材料吸收部分高能蓝光。 问题在于，这种“白”光仍然带有浓重的蓝色，这正是任何物种在进化过程中所期望的夜间颜色。

威斯敏斯特桥人行道上一盏历史悠久的三灯笼铸铁路灯在夜空的映衬下发出光芒。路易吉·阿万塔吉亚托 (Luigi Avantaggiato)

由于蓝光是可见光谱中第二高能的部分（紫光是最高的），它不仅照亮我们的街道并侵入我们的家庭。 蓝光比任何其他颜色更容易在大气中散射，这有助于在任何规模的每个城市上空形成朦胧、明亮的雾气，称为天空辉光。

4,000 至 6,500 开尔文范围内的“冷色”彩色 LED 以最低的成本提供最多的流明，因此大多数早期采用者安装了这些富含蓝色的白光。 好消息是 LED 技术不断进步，越来越多的社区开始选择蓝光较少的暖色路灯。 （例如，Phoenix 在 2020 年将 100,000 盏路灯改造为 2,700 K LED。） 当然，光污染不仅仅是 LED 造成的。 旧的照明技术也增加了眩光——尤其是明亮的白色金属卤化物灯——而且城市不愿意更换尚未损坏的东西。

但我们的主要失败不是技术问题。 而是我们还没有改变对夜间照明的看法。 我们使用 LED 技术就像使用旧光源一样。 结果，我们因加剧光污染而在很大程度上抵消了在减少能源消耗和碳排放方面所承诺的收益，并且到目前为止，我们还没有实现这一令人难以置信的机会。

光之城能做对吗？

在英吉利海峡对岸的巴黎，人们更明显地未能意识到 LED 的潜力。 与遭受德国炸弹重创的伦敦不同，奥斯曼男爵创建的美丽的 19 世纪巴黎在二战中基本上没有遭到破坏。 对于每年近 5000 万游客来说，这座建筑的美丽统一性是一眼就能认出来的。 但这座光之城的夜间氛围也是吸引人的一部分，人们对重新照亮其建筑物和纪念碑给予了广泛的关注。 例如，当我漫步走进卢浮宫的方正庭院时，我被一排排琥珀色 LED 灯震惊了，这些 LED 灯共同沿着宫殿外墙发出温暖的光芒。 当我看到埃菲尔铁塔时，先是沿着塞纳河从远处走过去，然后走近看，我发现自己凝视着篝火，该建筑的金属结构由超过 336 个高压钠灯泡发出琥珀色的光芒。

埃菲尔铁塔标志性的金色灯光于 1985 年落成。 336 个高压钠投影仪直接安装在纪念碑结构内部。Luigi Avantaggiato；埃菲尔铁塔金色灯光的灯光设计师：Pierre Bideau

尽管如此，这座城市的夜间照明还远未达到完美。 这座城市拥有数百万居民、数千家商店和餐馆以及 30 万盏路灯，总体而言是世界上最明亮的城市之一。 即使在塔的底部，明亮的白色 LED 灯也照亮了出售廉价贝雷帽和环法自行车赛饰品的非洲移民。 该市一直在用新的 LED 灯取代旧的钠灯路灯，取代了这座城市长期以来以没人愿意看到的明亮白色路灯而闻名的暖黄色调。

街头小贩在埃菲尔铁塔脚下展示纪念品。 他们的商品采用刺眼的白色 LED 系统照明，与照亮上方塔楼的温暖金色钠蒸气灯形成鲜明对比。

路易吉·阿万塔吉亚托

尽管如此，潜力就在这里。 2019年，法国出台了一项全国性法律，旨在降低光污染水平，对公共照明（防止光线投射到水平线以上）和商店等私人照明制定了规则，要求商店在凌晨1点后关闭外部和商店橱窗的灯光。 此外，越来越多的法国社区在午夜后调暗或关闭市政灯光，以节省能源并减少碳排放。 尽管全球光污染继续以每年近 10% 的速度增加，但法国已成功降低其总体水平。 巴黎萨克雷大学研究员 Chloé Beaudet 记录了当地的减光措施，发现人们普遍同意调暗或关灯的想法，主要是出于节能和生态考虑。

研究员 Chloé Beaudet 从附近的 Pont de l’Archevêché（大主教桥）眺望巴黎圣母院。Luigi Avantaggiato

“我发现生活在城市地区的人们接受这种政策，”她告诉我。 “他们说，好吧，我晚上作为行人并不真正使用公共空间，所以开灯有什么意义呢？” 对她来说，一个重要的收获是一种照明水平并不适合所有区域。 “我认为确实需要根据社区制定差异化的政策。”

巴黎圣母院大教堂的西立面经过修复后在夜间闪闪发光，建筑 LED 照明以温暖的白光照亮了三个纪念性的大门、玫瑰窗和双塔，保留了中世纪的石灰石细节。Luigi Avantaggiato

另一个积极的进展是，该国一直在尽量减少人造光，以创建旨在保护鸟类、蝙蝠和昆虫等夜间活动物种的生态走廊。 这些走廊相连且黑暗，减轻了对 30% 的脊椎动物和 60% 以上的夜间活动的无脊椎动物的干扰。 即使对于城市居民来说，这种黑色的灯光（“黑暗基础设施”）也有助于提高人们对控制光污染为何对地球生命如此重要的认识。 控制光污染的全国性法律、为城市不同部分提供不同照明的能力、保护生物多样性的黑暗走廊——这些正是 LED 带来的改变。

标志性的 I.M. 贝金字塔在卢浮宫博物馆拿破仑中庭的中心发出柔和的光芒，其温暖的 LED 照明流过几何玻璃和金属结构，与周围历史悠久的展馆在夜空的映衬下形成对称的框架。

路易吉·阿万塔吉亚托

这还不是全部。 几乎直到1920年，巴黎天文台的天文学家仍在凝视银河系。 这在今天是不可能的，但它可能再次发生。 尽管巴黎许多街道上现在都安装了明亮的白色 LED 路灯，但使用控制装置的 LED 网络可以在每晚降低照明水平，从而使法国首都上空再次可见银河系。 在此过程中，巴黎可能成为光明之城，为世界其他地区树立榜样。

新照明需要新思维

当我提到巴黎的这种景观时，西蒙·索普说道：“我认为我们应该渴望拥有能看到星星的城市。” “你只需要协调一切。”

伦敦徒步之旅即将结束时，索普从河边转向河岸街，带我沿着萨沃伊酒店后面狭窄的卡丁巷走下去，来到一根粗大灯柱顶部的煤气装置，这是 1870 年的原件。 一块小牌匾上写着：“威斯敏斯特市仅存的下水道气体破坏灯。” 索普解释说，粗杆隐藏着一根管道，可以让下水道中的甲烷在地幔处燃烧掉。 “可再生能源的早期例子，”他开玩笑说。

火橙色的火焰令人赏心悦目。 但即使是在这里，在城市旅游区一条没有车辆通行的狭窄小巷里，火焰也被附近未屏蔽的 LED 安全灯所淹没。 索普摇摇头。 “令人惊叹的是，有人能在这样的灯光下思考，‘太棒了，干得好。’”

游客们注视着韦伯专利下水道煤气灯，这是伦敦仅存的维多利亚时代下水道通风路灯，它照亮了斯特兰德街附近卡丁巷被雨水打湿的人行道。 该灯由约瑟夫·韦伯 (Joseph Webb) 于 19 世纪末发明，用于燃烧地下网络中的甲烷，该灯每天 24 小时运行。Luigi Avantaggiato

这就是当代夜间人工照明的症结所在。 我们知道如何提供良好的照明，而 LED 使我们能够做到这一点。 然而，尽管我们的技术已经进入 21 世纪，但我们对光明与黑暗、安全与保障的思考却常常停留在过去。 利用这项技术，我们可以做更多的事情。 我们可以通过多种方式重新照亮我们的夜晚，不仅可以减少能源和维护成本，还可以带来一系列好处，包括人类更健康的夜晚、夜行动物更安全的天空以及星星的恢复。

2026 年，这两座城市及其夜间人造光的故事是我们设计但尚未学会掌握的一项出色技术的故事。 简而言之，我们还没有改变对夜间人造光的看法，并更加深思熟虑和谨慎地使用它——正如我们所希望的那样。
